elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper an asymptotic expansion of the global error on the stepsize for partitioned linear multistep methods is proved.
elements.sent-2411-002: This provides a tool to analyse the behaviour of these integrators with respect to error growth with time and conservation of invariants.
elements.sent-2411-003: In particular, symmetric partitioned linear multistep methods with no common roots in their first characteristic polynomials, except unity, appear as efficient methods to approximate non-separable Hamiltonian systems since they can be explicit and show good long term behaviour at the same time.
elements.sent-2411-004: As a case study, a thorough analysis is given for small oscillations of the double pendulum problem, which is illustrated by numerical experiments.
elements.sent-2411-005: The advantages that geometric integrators offer when integrating ordinary differential problems with a certain structure are well-known \cite{HLW_geom,LR,SSC .}
elements.sent-2411-006: In particular, symplecticity and symmetry are always desirable properties of the integrators in order to emulate the qualitative behaviour of Hamiltonian and reversible systems and even to conserve or better approximate invariants of the problem.
elements.sent-2411-007: Nevertheless, when integrating general first-order systems, symplectic Runge-Kutta methods do necessarily need to be implicit.
elements.sent-2411-008: Just for special types of systems, explicit symplectic Runge-Kutta-type methods can be constructed.
elements.sent-2411-009: That is the case of second-order systems where the first-derivative does not turn up, for which there exist explicit symplectic Runge-Kutta-Nystr\"om methods and separable problems for which partitioned Runge-Kutta methods can be implemented in an explicit way.
elements.sent-2411-010: Even for some very special classes of nonseparable Hamiltonian problems, there exist some explicit symplectic integrators especifically constructed for them, like those in \cite{B,C,MQ,T .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, for general nonseparable Hamiltonian problems, we just know of the quite recent semiexplicit symplectic integrators in \cite{JO, O , which preserve quadratic invariants but are computationally not clearly more efficient than the classical implicit Gauss methods.}
elements.sent-2411-012: On the other hand, in spite of the fact that linear multistep methods (LMMs) cannot be symplectic \cite{ESS , the ones which are symmetric satisfy that their underlying one-step method is conjugate-symplectic \cite{Hairer .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: This is good since LMMs can be explicit and symmetric at the same time and a lot of effort has been made in the literature to develop and analyse these methods \cite{CD1, CD2, CSS, HL, QT .}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, the advantageous theoretical results which have been obtained for long-term integration just concern again either the integration of second-order differential systems with no first derivatives with symmetric LMMs especially designed for them (and with no double roots in their first characteristic polynomial \cite{CSS,HL ) either the integration of separable Hamiltonian systems with symmetric partitioned LMMs (PLMMs) where the first characteristic polynomials are not allowed to have common roots except for that which gives consistency \cite{CH .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In the case of LMMs for special 2nd-order systems, the analysis was firstly done in terms of asymptotic expansions of the global error and the conclusion was that, in some problems, like planetary motion ones, the propagation of error just in some specific direction led to a very slow error growth with time compared with other non-symmetric LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-016: On the contrary, general symmetric LMMs applied to the underlying first-order systems were shown to lead to exponential error growth with time, much worse than non-symmetric LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-017: Later on, an analysis on the same type of methods for 2nd-order systems was performed using modified differential equations and very advantageos results on the approximation of invariants were observed.
elements.sent-2411-018: In the case of PLMMs, the analysis was done in terms of modified differential equations and the conclusion was that, when the differential system is reversible, the modified differential equation which the smooth part of the numerical solution satisfies, is also reversible when the method is symmetric.
elements.sent-2411-019: Furthermore, when the Hamiltonian is separable with quadratic kinetic energy, the modified equation is Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-020: On the other hand, in separable Hamiltonian problems, the components associated to the parasitic roots are shown to remain bounded and small.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, no justification is given in \cite{CH on the behaviour of symmetric PLMMs with respect to invariants when the Hamiltonian is not quadratic and even less when the Hamiltonian is not separable.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The aim of this paper is thus to explain the good long term behaviour of symmetric PLMMs when integrating ordinary differential systems which do not necessarily correspond to separable Hamiltonian problems.
elements.sent-2411-023: In case this problem has invariants, an error analysis will be pursued.
elements.sent-2411-024: For that, we will focus on the asymptotic expansion of the global error, which is thoroughly studied for general PLMMs.
elements.sent-2411-025: We will illustrate the conclusions which can be drawn with our analysis in a particular example, that of a double pendulum, which corresponds to a non-separable Hamiltonian problem.
elements.sent-2411-026: We will see that in the case of small oscillations, our analysis allows to justify the good behaviour of symmetric PLMMs (with no common roots in their first characteristic polynomial except unity) against symmetric LMMs or non-symmetric LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-027: We advance that this advantageous behaviour can be seen and justified in other problems in the context of time integration of partial differential problems \cite{CD3 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: The paper is structured as follows: In Section 2, PLMMs are introduced and an asymptotic expansion for the global error on the stepsize is described in detail.
elements.sent-2411-029: For the sake of clarity, the proof of that is given in Appendix \ref{secA1 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In Section 3, the growth of error with time of the coefficients of that asymptotic expansion is analysed.
elements.sent-2411-031: For that, symmetry and the accuracy of the starting approximation are taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-032: These results are used in Section 4 in order to understand the behaviour in the numerical integration of those invariant quantities that the differential system may admit.
elements.sent-2411-033: Hamitonian systems and symmetric methods are again given particular consideration.
elements.sent-2411-034: Finally, a thorough analysis is performed for the case of small oscillations of a double pendulum, justifying why certain symmetric PLMMs perform so well in comparison with other non-symmetric PLMMs or symmetric non-partitioned LMMs.
elements.sent-2411-035: We end with some concluding remarks.
